clinicalStem: A 2-day-old male neonate is brought to the newborn nursery for routine assessment. He was born at 39 weeks to a 42-year-old mother via normal vaginal delivery. Birth weight is 2.8 kg. On examination, the infant has upslanting palpebral fissures, epicanthal folds, a flat nasal bridge, a single transverse palmar crease, and hypotonia. The tongue appears large and protrudes slightly. Echocardiography reveals an atrioventricular septal defect. The pediatrician explains to the parents that a chromosomal analysis is needed. Which karyotype result is MOST consistent with this clinical picture?
options: 46,XY with deletion of chromosome 5p
47,XXY
explanation: The constellation of upslanting palpebral fissures, flat nasal bridge, single palmar crease, hypotonia, macroglossia, and atrioventricular septal defect in a neonate born to an older mother is classic for Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome), confirmed by karyotype 47,XY,+21. Advanced maternal age (>35 years) is the strongest risk factor. AVSD is the most common cardiac defect in Down syndrome, occurring in about 40% of cases.
wrongAnswerExplanations: 45,X is Turner syndrome, presenting in females with short stature, webbed neck, and gonadal dysgenesis — not this male infant's features.
47,XY,+21 is correct: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) explains the dysmorphic features, hypotonia, and AVSD in this neonate of an older mother.
47,XY,+18 is Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome); it presents with overlapping fingers, rocker-bottom feet, micrognathia, and VSD/ASD — not AVSD or the facial features described.
46,XY with 5p deletion is Cri du chat syndrome, presenting with a high-pitched cat-like cry, microcephaly, and round face — does not fit.
47,XXY is Klinefelter syndrome, typically diagnosed in adolescence with tall stature, small testes, and gynecomastia — not neonatal dysmorphic features.
clinicalPearl: AVSD (endocardial cushion defect) is the hallmark cardiac lesion in Down syndrome; all neonates with Trisomy 21 should have echocardiography.
keyClue: Advanced maternal age, upslanting palpebral fissures, single palmar crease, AVSD, and hypotonia together are pathognomonic for Trisomy 21.
commonExamTrap: Confusing Trisomy 18 (overlapping fingers, IUGR, micrognathia) with Trisomy 21; remember AVSD + hypotonia + flat nasal bridge = Down syndrome.
missedConcept: Classic dysmorphic features and associated congenital heart defects of Trisomy 21.

clinicalStem: A 13-year-old girl is referred for short stature and primary amenorrhea. She is at the 2nd percentile for height and has not yet developed breast buds. On examination, she has a low posterior hairline, webbing of the neck, widely spaced nipples, and a broad shield-shaped chest. Her blood pressure is 145/90 mmHg. Serum FSH is markedly elevated, LH is elevated, and estradiol is very low. Karyotype reveals 45,X. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate initial management?
options: Start combined oral contraceptive pill immediately
Refer for renal ultrasound to screen for structural anomalies
Begin growth hormone therapy and plan estrogen replacement at age 14–15
Arrange urgent cardiac MRI to evaluate for aortic coarctation
Prescribe clomiphene citrate to stimulate ovulation
explanation: In Turner syndrome (45,X), the dual priorities are optimising final height with growth hormone and inducing puberty with low-dose estrogen replacement at age 12–15 to allow breast development and uterine growth while not compromising height. Growth hormone is initiated early (often age 4–8) but remains relevant here. Estrogen is introduced gradually to mimic normal puberty. While cardiac screening is important, the aortic coarctation would typically be detected earlier and is not the most pressing immediate step at this established diagnosis stage with hypertension already present.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Combined OCP delivers too much estrogen too abruptly and does not optimise height; it is not appropriate as first-line therapy.
Renal ultrasound is important (horseshoe kidney occurs in 30% of Turner syndrome) but is a screening step, not the most immediate management priority addressing growth and pubertal induction.
Growth hormone combined with planned estrogen replacement at 14–15 is correct: this is the standard approach to maximise height and induce puberty in Turner syndrome.

Cardiac MRI for aortic coarctation is important in Turner syndrome but should have been done at diagnosis; it is not the next step when estrogen deficiency and growth failure are the immediate clinical issues.
Clomiphene is used for ovulation induction in women with functional ovaries; Turner syndrome patients have streak gonads and cannot be stimulated to ovulate.
clinicalPearl: Turner syndrome = 45,X; manage with GH to optimise height, then low-dose estrogen titrated up for pubertal induction, followed by cyclic estrogen-progesterone.
keyClue: 45,X karyotype, elevated FSH/LH, very low estradiol, no breast development — primary hypogonadism requiring hormone replacement.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to cardiac imaging before addressing the immediate hormonal needs of growth and puberty induction in an already-diagnosed Turner patient.
missedConcept: Sequential management of Turner syndrome: GH first, then estrogen replacement timed to preserve height potential.

sourceLecture: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs
sourceFile: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs.pdf
clinicalStem: A 22-month-old boy is brought by his mother who is concerned that he is not talking. She reports he does not point at objects, does not wave bye-bye, rarely makes eye contact, and seems more interested in spinning the wheels of toy cars than playing with other children. He had previously said 'mama' and 'dada' at 10 months but stopped using these words around 14 months. His hearing test at birth was normal. On examination, he does not respond to his name consistently and does not use any words. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?
options: Reassure the mother that boys talk later than girls and review at age 3
Order an MRI brain to rule out structural causes
Refer immediately for formal developmental and autism evaluation and start speech therapy
Prescribe risperidone for self-stimulatory behaviors
Order chromosomal microarray as the sole next investigation
explanation: This child has multiple red flags for autism spectrum disorder (ASD): regression of language (stopped saying words), lack of pointing, no waving, poor eye contact, and restricted repetitive behaviors (wheel spinning). Language regression after 18 months is an absolute red flag requiring urgent developmental assessment. Early intensive intervention (ABA, speech therapy, occupational therapy) improves outcomes significantly, so referral should not be delayed. Reassurance is inappropriate when language regression and social withdrawal co-exist.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reassurance is dangerous when language regression has occurred; this is an absolute red flag requiring immediate evaluation, not watchful waiting.
MRI brain is not the first-line investigation for ASD; neuroimaging is only indicated if specific neurological signs suggest a structural lesion.
Referral for formal ASD evaluation plus initiation of speech therapy is the correct response — this is time-sensitive as early intervention significantly improves outcomes.
Risperidone addresses irritability/aggression in older children with ASD; it is not indicated before a formal diagnosis is made in a toddler.
Chromosomal microarray may be part of a workup but is not the sole or first step; establishing the developmental diagnosis and beginning therapy takes priority.
clinicalPearl: Language regression at any age is an absolute developmental red flag — never reassure; refer urgently for ASD evaluation and start early intervention without delay.
keyClue: Language regression (lost 'mama'/'dada') + no pointing + poor eye contact + repetitive object play = ASD red flags demanding immediate referral.
commonExamTrap: Normalising late speech in boys or waiting to 'see if he catches up' when language regression has occurred — regression is never normal.
missedConcept: Language regression after 18 months is an absolute red flag for ASD; early intervention before age 3 yields the best outcomes.

sourceLecture: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs
sourceFile: Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs.pdf
clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a school report of learning difficulties and behavioral problems including hyperactivity, hand-flapping, and difficulty with eye contact. His mother mentions his 8-year-old brother has similar problems and their maternal uncle was also 'slow.' On examination, the boy has a long narrow face, large ears, a prominent jaw, and macroorchidism. IQ testing reveals mild-to-moderate intellectual disability. Which of the following is the MOST likely underlying diagnosis and confirmatory investigation?
options: Trisomy 21; karyotype
Rett syndrome; MECP2 gene sequencing
Fragile X syndrome; FMR1 CGG repeat expansion testing
Angelman syndrome; methylation studies at 15q11-q13
Prader-Willi syndrome; FISH for 15q11-q13 deletion
explanation: Fragile X syndrome (FMR1 trinucleotide CGG repeat expansion on Xq27.3) is the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability. Classic features include long face, large protruding ears, prominent jaw, macro-orchidism after puberty, autism-like behaviors (hand-flapping, poor eye contact), and hyperactivity. The X-linked pattern explains why males are more severely affected and multiple males on the maternal side are involved. Confirmatory testing is molecular analysis of CGG repeat number in the FMR1 gene.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Trisomy 21 presents with different dysmorphic features (flat face, upslanting palpebral fissures, AVSD) and does not explain the X-linked family pattern.
Rett syndrome affects females almost exclusively and presents with hand-wringing (not hand-flapping), regression after normal development, and deceleration of head growth.
Fragile X syndrome with FMR1 CGG repeat expansion testing is correct — long face, large ears, macro-orchidism, X-linked inheritance, and autism-like behaviors are classic.
Angelman syndrome features a happy affect, seizures, ataxic gait, absent speech — not the male-predominant familial pattern with macro-orchidism described here.
Prader-Willi syndrome features obesity, hyperphagia, short stature, almond-shaped eyes, and small hands/feet — not macro-orchidism or the described dysmorphics.
clinicalPearl: Fragile X = most common inherited intellectual disability; remember long face + large ears + macro-orchidism + X-linked family pattern; confirm with FMR1 CGG repeat analysis.
keyClue: X-linked pattern of intellectual disability + large ears + macro-orchidism + hand-flapping = Fragile X syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Confusing Fragile X with Down syndrome; Down syndrome has flat facies and AVSD, while Fragile X has elongated facies and macro-orchidism.
missedConcept: Fragile X syndrome is the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability, caused by FMR1 CGG trinucleotide repeat expansion.

clinicalStem: A 14-hour-old neonate born at 38 weeks via spontaneous vaginal delivery is brought to the neonatal unit by the midwife who notes the baby is grunting, has subcostal and intercostal recessions, and is pale with mottled skin. Temperature is 38.9°C, heart rate 195 bpm, respiratory rate 68/min, and oxygen saturation is 88% on room air. The mother had a urine infection treated at 36 weeks and was GBS-positive on a vaginal swab taken at 34 weeks; she received only one dose of intrapartum penicillin. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate IMMEDIATE management?
options: Reassure and observe for 6 hours; sepsis is unlikely in a term neonate
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics (benzylpenicillin + gentamicin) after taking blood cultures, and support airway, breathing, and circulation
Await full blood count and CRP result before starting antibiotics
Give oral amoxicillin and discharge with community midwife follow-up
Perform lumbar puncture immediately before any other intervention
explanation: This neonate has signs of early-onset sepsis (EOS) — likely GBS — with fever, tachycardia, respiratory distress, desaturation, and mottling within 24 hours of birth. Risk factors include inadequate intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (only one dose; at least two doses 4 hours apart are required). The immediate priority is ABCDE stabilisation followed by blood cultures and prompt IV antibiotics (benzylpenicillin + gentamicin per NICE/WHO EOS guidelines). Delaying antibiotics waiting for results is dangerous in a deteriorating neonate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reassurance is inappropriate and dangerous; this neonate meets clinical criteria for sepsis with respiratory failure and haemodynamic instability.
Stabilise airway/breathing/circulation, take blood cultures, and start benzylpenicillin + gentamicin immediately — this is correct and must not be delayed.
Waiting for blood results before starting antibiotics is dangerous; in neonatal sepsis, antibiotics should be given within 1 hour of clinical suspicion regardless of laboratory results.
Oral antibiotics are wholly inadequate for neonatal sepsis with respiratory compromise; IV antibiotics and inpatient monitoring are mandatory.
Lumbar puncture should be performed when the infant is stabilised; it must never be the first intervention in a haemodynamically unstable neonate.
clinicalPearl: In early-onset neonatal sepsis, do not delay antibiotics for investigation results — take cultures and start benzylpenicillin + gentamicin within 1 hour of clinical suspicion.
keyClue: Tachycardia, respiratory distress, mottling, and fever at 14 hours of life + inadequate GBS prophylaxis = early-onset neonatal sepsis requiring immediate antibiotics.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for lab results (FBC, CRP, blood culture sensitivities) before starting antibiotics in a clinically septic neonate — time to antibiotics is the key outcome determinant.
missedConcept: Early-onset neonatal sepsis is a medical emergency; antibiotics must start within 1 hour of recognition, not after investigations.
redFlagReason: Neonatal sepsis with respiratory failure and haemodynamic compromise is immediately life-threatening; delayed antibiotics are associated with rapid deterioration and death.

clinicalStem: A 3-week-old female neonate is referred with poor feeding, lethargy, and a bulging fontanelle. She was born at 37 weeks to a mother who kept cats throughout pregnancy and reported a flu-like illness at 18 weeks of gestation that resolved without treatment. On examination, the infant is microcephalic, has bilateral chorioretinitis on fundoscopy, jaundice, and hepatosplenomegaly. Head CT reveals intracranial calcifications scattered throughout the brain parenchyma. Which of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Congenital cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection
Congenital rubella syndrome
Congenital toxoplasmosis
Neonatal herpes simplex encephalitis
Congenital syphilis
explanation: The classic tetrad of congenital toxoplasmosis is chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus/intracranial calcifications, intracranial calcifications (diffuse, throughout cortex), and hepatosplenomegaly. The history of cat contact (Toxoplasma gondii oocysts in cat feces) and maternal flu-like illness during pregnancy strongly supports primary maternal infection leading to transplacental transmission. Diffuse scattered calcifications (vs periventricular in CMV) are the radiological hallmark.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Congenital CMV causes periventricular calcifications (not diffuse), sensorineural hearing loss, and is the most common congenital infection — but scattered parenchymal calcifications with cat exposure favors toxoplasmosis.
Congenital rubella presents with cataracts, patent ductus arteriosus, sensorineural deafness, and blueberry muffin rash — chorioretinitis and diffuse calcifications are not its hallmark.
Congenital toxoplasmosis is correct: diffuse intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis, hepatosplenomegaly, and cat exposure during pregnancy are pathognomonic.
Neonatal HSV encephalitis presents acutely with seizures, vesicular rash, and temporal lobe changes — not congenital calcifications or chorioretinitis.
Congenital syphilis presents with snuffles, rash (coppery), periostitis on X-ray, hepatosplenomegaly, and saddle-nose — chorioretinitis with diffuse intracranial calcifications is not characteristic.
clinicalPearl: Congenital toxoplasmosis classic triad: chorioretinitis + diffuse intracranial calcifications + hydrocephalus; cat contact during pregnancy is the key epidemiological clue.
keyClue: Diffuse (not periventricular) intracranial calcifications + chorioretinitis + cat exposure = congenital toxoplasmosis.
commonExamTrap: Confusing CMV (periventricular calcifications + SNHL) with toxoplasmosis (diffuse calcifications + chorioretinitis); location of calcifications is the distinguishing radiological clue.
missedConcept: TORCH infections have overlapping presentations; calcification distribution and fundoscopic findings are key discriminators between CMV and toxoplasmosis.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old boy born prematurely at 28 weeks (birth weight 980 g) is referred by his pediatrician for motor concerns. He was slow to sit (sat independently at 12 months) and has not yet walked. His parents note that he stands on his toes and tends to scissor his legs when held upright. On examination, he has increased tone in all four limbs, more prominent in the legs, brisk deep tendon reflexes, and bilateral ankle clonus. Cranial MRI shows periventricular leukomalacia. Which of the following best describes his condition and the MOST appropriate initial intervention?
options: Duchenne muscular dystrophy; start corticosteroids
Spastic diplegic cerebral palsy; refer for physiotherapy and consider botulinum toxin for equinus deformity
Hereditary spastic paraplegia; genetic testing and supportive care
Spinal muscular atrophy type II; start nusinersen
Ataxic cerebral palsy; refer for occupational therapy only
explanation: Preterm birth at 28 weeks with periventricular leukomalacia (PVL), delayed motor milestones, increased tone predominantly in legs, scissoring gait, toe-walking, brisk DTRs, and ankle clonus are classic for spastic diplegic cerebral palsy. PVL selectively damages the periventricular white matter fibres that carry corticospinal tract signals to the legs. Initial management is physiotherapy to prevent contractures plus botulinum toxin A injections for dynamic equinus (toe-walking) to improve gait before orthopaedic surgery is considered.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Duchenne muscular dystrophy presents with proximal weakness, calf pseudohypertrophy, Gower sign, and elevated CK — not spasticity, clonus, or PVL on MRI.
Spastic diplegic cerebral palsy with physiotherapy and botulinum toxin is correct — preterm birth, PVL, and lower-limb predominant UMN signs are the diagnostic triad.
Hereditary spastic paraplegia is a genetic disorder with slower progression and no PVL; it would not explain acute neonatal brain injury in a preterm infant.
SMA type II presents with hypotonia, areflexia, and tongue fasciculations — the opposite neurological picture (flaccid, not spastic); nusinersen is not indicated.
Ataxic cerebral palsy features cerebellar signs (intention tremor, dysmetria, wide-based gait) — not spasticity, clonus, or periventricular leukomalacia.
clinicalPearl: Spastic diplegia is the most common CP subtype in preterm infants; periventricular leukomalacia is the underlying lesion, and botulinum toxin + physiotherapy is first-line for dynamic equinus.
keyClue: Preterm + PVL on MRI + spasticity predominantly in legs + toe-walking = spastic diplegic cerebral palsy.
commonExamTrap: Confusing hypotonic SMA (flaccid, areflexia) with spastic CP (hypertonic, hyperreflexia); the direction of tone and reflexes distinguishes UMN from LMN pathology.
missedConcept: Periventricular leukomalacia in preterm neonates is the most common cause of spastic diplegia; early physiotherapy prevents contracture progression.

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old girl with known spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy (GMFCS level IV) is seen in clinic. Her parents report she has increasing difficulty swallowing, excessive drooling that causes social embarrassment, recurrent chest infections, and severe lower-limb muscle spasms at night that disrupt her sleep. On examination, she has generalised spasticity, scissoring posture, and a tongue-thrust with drooling. Her chest X-ray shows right lower lobe consolidation. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate management combination?
options: Intrathecal baclofen pump for spasticity; oral glycopyrrolate or botulinum toxin to submandibular glands for sialorrhea; and chest physiotherapy for aspiration pneumonia
Oral diazepam alone for spasticity and reassure parents about drooling
Selective dorsal rhizotomy as the first surgical option and no treatment for drooling
Botulinum toxin to limb muscles only; no treatment for respiratory complication
Tracheostomy as the first intervention for recurrent chest infections
explanation: Spastic quadriplegic CP at GMFCS IV requires a multidisciplinary approach. Intrathecal baclofen is the gold standard for severe generalised spasticity unresponsive to oral agents, as it delivers baclofen directly to the intrathecal space with fewer systemic side effects. Sialorrhea in CP is effectively managed with glycopyrrolate (antimuscarinic) or botulinum toxin injections to salivary glands. Recurrent aspiration pneumonia due to oromotor dysfunction requires active treatment with chest physio and modified feeding strategies. All three problems must be addressed simultaneously.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Intrathecal baclofen for spasticity + glycopyrrolate/BTX for drooling + chest physio for aspiration pneumonia is correct — addresses all three active clinical problems appropriately.
Oral diazepam causes significant sedation and tolerance; it is not suitable for long-term spasticity management, and reassuring about drooling ignores aspiration risk.
Selective dorsal rhizotomy benefits ambulant children (GMFCS I-III) and is not appropriate as first intervention in GMFCS IV; ignoring drooling leaves aspiration risk unaddressed.
Treating only limb spasticity without addressing aspiration pneumonia neglects a potentially life-threatening complication of oromotor dysfunction.
Tracheostomy is a last resort; treating the underlying aspiration and spasticity first is the appropriate escalating approach.
clinicalPearl: In severe quadriplegic CP, intrathecal baclofen targets spasticity, botulinum toxin or glycopyrrolate targets sialorrhea, and aspiration pneumonia must always be actively managed — multidisciplinary care is essential.
keyClue: GMFCS IV + recurrent chest infections + severe sialorrhea + generalised spasticity = multi-system CP complications requiring comprehensive simultaneous management.
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on spasticity and ignoring sialorrhea/aspiration, or choosing oral diazepam for severe generalised spasticity when intrathecal baclofen is indicated.
missedConcept: Severe quadriplegic CP requires concurrent management of spasticity, oromotor dysfunction causing sialorrhea, and pulmonary consequences of aspiration.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 10-minute generalised tonic-clonic seizure, now post-ictal. His mother reports he has had infantile spasms since 6 months of age. On examination, there are three hypopigmented ash-leaf macules on the trunk, a shagreen patch over the lumbar spine, and multiple periungual fibromas on the toes. Brain MRI reveals cortical tubers and subependymal nodules. Renal ultrasound shows bilateral angiomyolipomas. Which of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Neurofibromatosis type 1
Sturge-Weber syndrome
Tuberous sclerosis complex
Von Hippel-Lindau disease
Incontinentia pigmenti
explanation: Tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC) is caused by mutations in TSC1 (hamartin) or TSC2 (tuberin) and is characterised by hamartomas in multiple organs. The diagnostic features here include ash-leaf macules (hypopigmented, best seen under Wood's lamp), shagreen patch (connective tissue naevus), periungual fibromas, cortical tubers, subependymal nodules on MRI, and renal angiomyolipomas. Infantile spasms are the most common early seizure type in TSC, occurring in 30–40% of patients.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Neurofibromatosis type 1 features cafe-au-lait macules, Lisch nodules, neurofibromas, and optic gliomas — not ash-leaf macules, shagreen patch, or cortical tubers.

Sturge-Weber syndrome presents with a port-wine stain in the trigeminal distribution, ipsilateral glaucoma, and leptomeningeal angiomas with tram-track calcifications — no ash-leaf or shagreen features.
Tuberous sclerosis complex is correct: the combination of ash-leaf macules, shagreen patch, periungual fibromas, cortical tubers, subependymal nodules, and renal angiomyolipomas is diagnostic.
Von Hippel-Lindau disease features retinal and cerebellar haemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and phaeochromocytoma — not the cutaneous or cortical features described.
Incontinentia pigmenti affects females (X-linked dominant, lethal in males), presenting with blistering then verrucous lesions then hyperpigmentation — not ash-leaf macules or cortical tubers.
clinicalPearl: Tuberous sclerosis = ash-leaf macules + shagreen patch + periungual fibromas + cortical tubers + subependymal nodules + renal angiomyolipomas; infantile spasms are the earliest seizure type.
keyClue: Ash-leaf macules + shagreen patch + periungual fibromas + cortical tubers/subependymal nodules on MRI = tuberous sclerosis complex.
commonExamTrap: Confusing NF1 (café-au-lait spots, neurofibromas) with TSC (ash-leaf macules, shagreen patch); remember TSC skin lesions are hypopigmented or fibrous, not café-au-lait.
missedConcept: TSC is a multi-organ hamartoma syndrome; the combination of neurological (cortical tubers), dermatological (ash-leaf, shagreen, fibromas), and renal (angiomyolipomas) features is diagnostic.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old girl is referred to pediatric neurology with concern about NF1. She has six café-au-lait macules each larger than 5 mm in diameter, freckling in both axillae, and two soft subcutaneous tumors along peripheral nerves confirmed as neurofibromas on biopsy. Her mother and maternal aunt also have multiple café-au-lait spots. She recently started complaining of blurring of vision in the left eye. Which of the following investigations is MOST urgently indicated?
options: Skin biopsy of a café-au-lait macule
MRI brain and orbits with gadolinium to evaluate for optic pathway glioma
EEG to exclude seizure focus
Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis
Chromosomal karyotype to confirm NF1 gene location
explanation: This child meets NF1 diagnostic criteria (>6 café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, two neurofibromas, and a first-degree relative with NF1). The new symptom of visual blurring in a child with NF1 is a red flag for optic pathway glioma (OPG), the most common CNS tumour in NF1, occurring in 15–20% of patients, usually in the first decade. MRI brain and orbits with gadolinium is the investigation of choice to identify and characterise OPG before irreversible visual loss occurs.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Skin biopsy of a café-au-lait macule adds nothing to the diagnosis, which is already established clinically; it will not identify OPG.
MRI brain and orbits with gadolinium is correct — new visual symptoms in a confirmed NF1 patient mandate urgent imaging to exclude optic pathway glioma.
EEG is relevant if seizures are reported, but visual loss in NF1 should prompt urgent orbital and cranial MRI before EEG, as OPG is the critical diagnosis to exclude.
Lumbar puncture has no diagnostic role in assessing visual loss from a structural tumour; it is not indicated here and could be dangerous if raised ICP is present.
Chromosomal karyotype does not confirm NF1 (caused by a point mutation/deletion in NF1 gene at 17q11.2, not a chromosomal anomaly visible on standard karyotype).
clinicalPearl: All children with NF1 who develop visual symptoms require urgent MRI orbits/brain to exclude optic pathway glioma — the most common CNS tumour in NF1.
keyClue: NF1 + new visual blurring = optic pathway glioma until proven otherwise; MRI orbits/brain with gadolinium is urgent.
commonExamTrap: Ordering an EEG for visual symptoms in NF1 instead of MRI; visual loss demands structural imaging, not electrophysiology.
missedConcept: Optic pathway glioma affects 15–20% of NF1 patients and can cause irreversible visual loss; visual symptoms must trigger urgent MRI.

clinicalStem: A 10-day-old neonate is brought to the emergency department with a 48-hour history of poor feeding, lethargy, and temperature of 38.2°C. His mother has a history of recurrent genital herpes simplex infection but had no active lesions at the time of delivery. On examination, the neonate is irritable, has a bulging fontanelle, and a generalised vesicular rash over the scalp and trunk. Blood glucose is 3.0 mmol/L. An LP shows CSF with 120 white cells (predominantly lymphocytes), protein 1.8 g/L, and normal glucose. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate IMMEDIATE treatment?
options: Start acyclovir IV 20 mg/kg/dose every 8 hours immediately
Wait for HSV PCR result from CSF before starting any antiviral
Start oral valacyclovir and observe in the ward
Give ceftriaxone only and monitor for 24 hours
Administer IV immunoglobulin as the primary treatment
explanation: Neonatal HSV encephalitis (CNS disease) is life-threatening with mortality >50% if untreated. The triad of vesicular rash, CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with raised protein, and a mother with HSV history mandates immediate IV acyclovir 20 mg/kg/dose every 8 hours without waiting for PCR confirmation. Delay in treatment is directly associated with neurodevelopmental sequelae and death. The vesicular rash is present in ~60% of CNS disease and is a critical clue. IV acyclovir must be started empirically.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IV acyclovir 20 mg/kg/dose every 8 hours immediately is correct and life-saving — neonatal HSV encephalitis must be treated empirically without awaiting confirmatory PCR results.
Waiting for PCR results before starting acyclovir is dangerous; PCR may take 24–48 hours and untreated HSV encephalitis carries >50% mortality and severe neurological sequelae.
Oral valacyclovir has no role in acute neonatal HSV encephalitis; only IV acyclovir achieves sufficient CNS levels.
Ceftriaxone alone misses the HSV diagnosis; while bacterial meningitis must also be covered, acyclovir is equally urgent and must not be omitted.
IV immunoglobulin is not the standard treatment for neonatal HSV; acyclovir is the specific antiviral of choice.
clinicalPearl: Start IV acyclovir 20 mg/kg/dose TDS immediately in any neonate with vesicular rash + CSF pleocytosis, regardless of HSV PCR result — never wait for confirmation.
keyClue: Vesicular rash + CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis + raised protein + maternal HSV history = neonatal HSV encephalitis → empirical IV acyclovir immediately.
commonExamTrap: Delaying acyclovir until PCR confirms HSV; in neonatal HSV encephalitis, empirical treatment must begin at clinical suspicion, not after lab confirmation.
missedConcept: Neonatal HSV encephalitis can occur even without active maternal lesions at delivery; a vesicular rash with CSF changes mandates immediate empirical IV acyclovir.
redFlagReason: Neonatal HSV encephalitis is rapidly fatal without immediate IV acyclovir; delay beyond clinical suspicion is associated with irreversible brain damage and death.

clinicalStem: A 6-week-old boy is referred after an abnormal newborn screening (heel-prick) result. He was born at term, birth weight 3.4 kg, and has been exclusively breastfed. The parents report no concerns. He is gaining weight well, smiling, and tracking. Serum phenylalanine level is markedly elevated at 1200 µmol/L (normal <120 µmol/L). Urine pterins are normal, excluding tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) deficiency. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: Reassure parents; elevated phenylalanine on heel-prick is a common false positive requiring no further action
Start a phenylalanine-restricted diet immediately with regular monitoring of Phe levels and neurodevelopment
Initiate a gluten-free diet as first-line dietary intervention
Start sapropterin dihydrochloride (BH4 analogue) as primary treatment without dietary changes
Withhold all feeds and await neurological symptoms before starting treatment
explanation: Classic phenylketonuria (PKU) is caused by phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency, leading to toxic accumulation of phenylalanine causing irreversible intellectual disability if untreated. Newborn screening enables pre-symptomatic detection. Treatment is immediate dietary restriction of phenylalanine (low-Phe formula supplemented with phenylalanine-free amino acid mixture) with regular monitoring of serum Phe levels, growth, and neurodevelopment. Starting treatment before neurological damage occurs is the goal of screening. BH4 is only used in BH4-responsive PKU, which has been excluded here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Dismissing the result as a false positive is dangerous; a confirmatory Phe level of 1200 µmol/L (10× normal) on serum testing confirms classic PKU requiring immediate treatment.
A phenylalanine-restricted diet with regular Phe monitoring is correct — initiated immediately after confirmation to prevent irreversible intellectual disability.
A gluten-free diet has no role in PKU; PKU requires phenylalanine restriction, not gluten avoidance.
Sapropterin (BH4 analogue) is indicated for BH4-responsive PKU; this patient has normal urine pterins, excluding BH4 deficiency, so dietary management is primary.
Waiting for neurological symptoms means allowing phenylalanine to accumulate and damage the developing brain — the entire point of newborn screening is to treat before symptoms appear.
clinicalPearl: PKU must be treated immediately on newborn screening confirmation — do not wait for symptoms; a low-phenylalanine diet started in the first weeks of life prevents intellectual disability.
keyClue: Markedly elevated serum phenylalanine on newborn screen with normal BH4 pathway = classic PKU; start low-Phe diet immediately.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing BH4 (sapropterin) when BH4 deficiency has already been excluded by normal urine pterins; BH4 is only for BH4-responsive PKU.
missedConcept: The entire benefit of PKU newborn screening is pre-symptomatic treatment; any delay or mismanagement results in preventable, irreversible intellectual disability.
